title: Regional Fee Schedule Comparison
resolved_text:
Memorandum
DATE:
TO:
February 2, 2026
Mayor and Council
THROUGH: Jackie Bryant, City Manager
FROM: Lynsey Hatfield, Budget Manager
DEPT: Finance
SUBJECT: Regional Fee Schedule Comparison
Process Overview
Pursuant to Council's request and in preparation for the Fiscal Year 2026/27 budget cycle, staff prepared a regional fee comparison of the City of Reno's adopted Fiscal Year 2025/26 fees and Washoe County, the City of Sparks, and Carson City fees. This information is provided in advance of the FY26/27 budget workshops to support Council's understanding of the City of Reno's fee structure within a regional context and to inform broader discussions related to long-term financial planning and service delivery.
The City of Reno's budget development process begins each year with departmental planning during November and December, with an emphasis on understanding Council priorities, assessing operational sustainability, and maintaining financial stability. As part of this process, fee schedules are reviewed annually to evaluate whether fee schedules continue to align with service costs, policy objectives, and community expectations. Regular fee review allows Council to consider whether fees for services should fully recover costs, partially recover costs, or be subsidized by the General Fund.
The attached document (Attachment A – FY25/26 Regional Fee Schedule Comparison) provides a comprehensive overview of the City of Reno's current fee schedule and presents corresponding fees from neighboring jurisdictions where comparable services exist. In cases where a direct comparison is not available, "N/A" or similar indicators are used to reflect differences in service offerings or fee structures.

Key Insights and Usefulness of Regional Comparison
The purpose of this comparison is to provide transparency and context regarding how the City of Reno's fees relate to those of surrounding agencies, recognizing that alignment, cost recovery, and equity considerations may differ by jurisdiction. Regional benchmarking can help identify broad trends, areas of relative alignment, and categories where fee-setting approaches diverge due to policy or operational choices.
While regional comparisons do not establish a single "correct" fee level, regional comparisons provide useful reference points that can inform discussions related to competitiveness, cost recovery philosophy, and service delivery expectations.
Interpretation Guidance
Direct comparison of municipal fees across the region is inherently challenging due to significant differences in how agencies structure, calculate, and apply fees. The City of Reno's fee schedule is more granular and detailed than those of some regional partners, with specific fees tied to defined services or license types. For example, the City of Reno assesses distinct business license fees by activity, while other jurisdictions rely more heavily on gross-receipts-based models. While this level of detail can support accurate cost recovery, this level of detail can also increase administrative complexity and make side-by-side comparisons less straightforward.
Additional challenges arise from differences in fee methodologies, including the use of flat fees, valuation-based fees, hourly rates, or a combination of approaches. Jurisdictions also vary in whether plan review, inspections, and administrative costs are bundled into a single fee or assessed separately. As a result, similar projects may experience different total costs depending on project characteristics, review complexity, and local practices, even when published fees appear higher or lower.
Regional comparisons are further influenced by variations in service levels, staffing models, regulatory frameworks, adopted codes, and local policy objectives. In some cases, an agency with lower published fees may offset costs through greater General Fund support, while others may rely more heavily on user fees to fund service delivery. Consequently, regional fee comparisons are best understood as contextual benchmarks rather than direct equivalency assessments.
Looking Forward
Fees are generally designed to offset the cost of providing fee-supported services, including personnel, overhead, technology, and regulatory compliance. Personnel-related costs such as salaries, benefits, training, and overtime, along with expenses for software, professional services, materials, and fuel, continue to affect service delivery costs. Many fee-supported activities, including plan review, inspections, and enforcement, also contribute directly to public safety and regulatory compliance, making adequate resourcing an important consideration.
Regular, annual review of the fee schedule provides Council with an opportunity to understand the long-term sustainability of fee-supported programs, set cost recovery or subsidization goals, and avoid the need for infrequent, larger adjustments. This approach supports transparent financial planning and informed decision-making while recognizing the operational and policy factors that influence municipal fee structures.
Clear policy direction from Council regarding cost recovery versus subsidization goals is an important component of the annual fee review and budget development process. Establishing whether specific services are intended to fully recover costs, partially recover costs, or remain subsidized by the General Fund provides a consistent framework for staff analysis and recommendations. This direction helps guide staff as staff evaluate fee schedules, assess fiscal impacts, and develop options for consideration during upcoming budget workshops. Clarifying these policy objectives in advance supports transparent decision-making and ensures that future recommendations align with Council priorities and broader financial planning goals.

* There is no fee to appeal a civil penalty notice for a code violation to the Administrative Hearing Office (Washoe County uses an Administrative Enforcement ordinance to conduct civil enforcement, WCC Chapter 125), unless the respondent loses the appeal. If the respondent "loses" at the appeal hearing, then the respondent owes the Administrative Hearing Office $50 and also whatever penalty amount was upheld. Civil fines for code violations for 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and subsequent penalties are $100, $200, and $400, cumulative. The fine amount doesn't matter what type of violation the fine is for (unless the violation is a Short Term Rental violation, then the fines are in the thousands). Washoe County could issue daily fines, but usually fines are issued with 30-day comply by timelines.
* Parking appeals require the constituent to deposit the amount issued on the citation.

fields.tldr: At Council's request, the Finance Department prepared a regional fee schedule comparison of Reno's adopted FY2025/26 fees against those of Washoe County, the City of Sparks, and Carson City to inform the FY2026/27 budget cycle. The memo cautions that direct fee comparisons are difficult due to differences in fee structures, methodologies, and service delivery models across jurisdictions. The attached comparison (Attachment A) covers fees across departments including Building, Business License, Parks, Police, Public Works, and Utilities. Council is encouraged to provide clear policy direction on cost recovery versus General Fund subsidization goals ahead of upcoming budget workshops.
fields.key_stats: Fee categories compared include City Wide, Building (Fire, Other, Permit, Valuation), Business License, Development Services, Enforcement, Fire Prevention Permits, Maintenance and Operations, Municipal Court, Parks and Recreation, Police, Public Works, Special Events, and Utility Services. Sample fees: standard copies $0.05/page (Reno) vs. $0.50/page (Sparks); return check fee $39 (Reno) vs. $25 (Sparks/Carson City); administrative appeal $100 (Reno) vs. N/A (Sparks/Carson City); land use appeal $1,200 (Reno) vs. $1,043–$1,448 (Washoe County) vs. $250 + $60/hour (Carson City); standard hourly rate for plan check $131.25 (Reno) vs. $100 (Sparks) vs. $90 (Washoe County); fire alarm systems $2.00/device (Reno) vs. $69.75 (Washoe County).
fields.key_decisions: Council is asked to provide policy direction on whether specific fee-supported services should fully recover costs, partially recover costs, or be subsidized by the General Fund, to guide staff analysis and recommendations for the FY2026/27 budget workshops.
fields.action_items: Staff will use Council's cost recovery versus subsidization policy direction to evaluate fee schedules, assess fiscal impacts, and develop options for consideration during upcoming FY2026/27 budget workshops.
